evals.prompt: Create C4 diagrams for our internet banking system showing context and containers.
evals.expected_output: A C4 document with System Context and Container levels as Mermaid diagrams plus element catalogs.

deterministic_checks.description: Emits a Mermaid C4Context (Level 1) fenced block
Emits a Mermaid C4Container (Level 2) fenced block
Declares at least one Person actor
Declares a system or container boundary
MIF frontmatter declares type: semantic

expectations: Element catalog beneath each diagram names every actor, system, and container with its single responsibility, not just repeating the diagram code
Relationships in the diagrams are labeled with what flows between elements and how, not left as bare unlabeled arrows

evals.prompt: My C4 diagram is just a box for every microservice. What's wrong?
evals.expected_output: Explains the abstraction levels and that Context/Container views need actors and boundaries, not a flat service map.

deterministic_checks.description: Response calls out the missing people/actors
Response calls out the missing system boundary
Response frames the problem as an abstraction-level issue

expectations: Identifies that mixing containers and components (or services) into one flat diagram is the core problem, not just that it looks cluttered
Recommends starting at Level 1 (System Context) and zooming in one level at a time rather than redrawing the same flat diagram

evals.prompt: What are the four levels of the C4 model and which do I usually need?
evals.expected_output: Lists Context, Container, Component, Code and notes Code is usually omitted.

deterministic_checks.description: Names the Context level
Names the Container level
Names the Component level
Names the Code level
Notes the Code level is usually omitted or generated on demand

expectations: States that each level zooms into the previous one rather than describing the four as unrelated, interchangeable views
Frames Level 3 (Component) as the typical stopping point for most systems, not Level 4

evals.prompt: Upgrade this C4 model so a tool can tell whether the view is stale, what kind of document it is, and where it came from.
evals.expected_output: A Level 3 C4 document adding temporal validity, an architecture-view ontology type, W3C-PROV provenance, and a typed relationship, while keeping the Mermaid C4 diagrams.

deterministic_checks.description: Adds temporal validity frontmatter
Sets an architecture-view ontology type
Adds W3C-PROV provenance
Adds a typed relates-to relationship
Keeps the Mermaid C4 diagrams intact

expectations: Keeps type: semantic unchanged while climbing to L3 rather than switching the document's fundamental type
References mif-validate --level 3 and a lossless markdown<->JSON-LD round-trip, not just the added frontmatter fields

evals.prompt: Here's my C4 doc: one diagram has Person, external system boxes, AND internal service components all together, with no boundary box anywhere. Is this valid C4?
evals.expected_output: Identifies the mixed abstraction levels and missing boundary as the core defects and recommends splitting into separate Context/Container/Component diagrams.

deterministic_checks.description: Response identifies the mixed abstraction levels
Response calls out the missing boundary
Response recommends splitting into separate per-level diagrams

expectations: Explains the fix is one diagram per abstraction level rather than a single merged picture with everything on it
Calls out the missing system/container boundary as a Level 1 requirement, not merely a stylistic suggestion

evals.prompt: We already have Context and Container diagrams for the checkout service. Now drill into the 'Order API' container and show its components.
evals.expected_output: A Level 3 Component diagram scoped to the Order API container, naming its components with a matching container name and an element catalog of responsibilities.

deterministic_checks.description: Emits a Mermaid C4Component (Level 3) fenced block
Declares at least one Component
Keeps the container name consistent with the prior Level 2 diagram

expectations: The Level 3 diagram stays scoped to components inside Order API only, not other containers from the checkout service's Level 2 view
Element catalog lists each component's single responsibility, matching the pattern used at the Context and Container levels
